text.title: Jenkins Basics Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the jenkins-basics methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every new Jenkinsfile MUST use Declarative syntax (`pipeline { ... }`). Scripted blocks are only allowed inside `script { }` sections when Declarative cannot express the logic, with a code comment explaining why.
rule.rationale: Declarative pipelines support syntax validation at load time, restart-from-stage, and the `post` section for structured cleanup. Scripted pipelines provide no static validation; errors appear only at runtime.

rule.statement: The Jenkins controller MUST have 0 executors. All material work (clone, build, test) runs on Kubernetes / Docker / label-bound agents.
rule.rationale: Builds on the controller consume scheduling and plugin-processing resources, causing job-queue delays and OOM kills of Jenkins itself. They also have direct filesystem access to the credentials store — a security violation.

rule.statement: All controller configuration (security realm, authorization, node definitions, shared libraries, credentials) MUST be defined in a JCasC YAML file in source control. UI-driven changes are forbidden — any drift MUST be re-applied via JCasC.
rule.rationale: Manual Jenkins configuration is not reproducible. After a DR restore, JCasC re-applies the full config in minutes; without it, rebuilding a controller takes hours of manual clicks and is unauditable.

rule.statement: Secrets MUST be injected via the Credentials plugin (`withCredentials([...]) { ... }` or `credentials('id')`). Hard-coded credentials in Jenkinsfiles are forbidden. `sh "echo ${secret}"` interpolation is forbidden; use `sh '''echo "$SECRET"'''` with env-var binding.
rule.rationale: Hard-coded credentials end up in Git and build logs. Groovy `${}` interpolation inside `sh "..."` is a shell-injection footgun and leaks secrets into the process tree.

rule.statement: Every pipeline MUST set `options { timeout(time: 30, unit: 'MINUTES'); buildDiscarder(logRotator(numToKeepStr: '10')); disableConcurrentBuilds() }` (or stricter project-specific values). Every pipeline MUST clean its workspace in `post { always { cleanWs() } }`.
rule.rationale: Pipelines without timeouts hang on stuck processes and consume executor slots. Without buildDiscarder, accumulated build artifacts and logs fill the controller disk. Without cleanWs, agents accumulate stale workspaces.

rule.statement: For dynamic agent pools, use `agent { kubernetes { yaml '...' } }` with explicit `resources.requests` + `resources.limits` per container, or `agent { docker { image '...' args '...' } }` for single-container builds. `agent any` is forbidden in production pipelines.
rule.rationale: Kubernetes agents are created on demand and destroyed after the build, eliminating stale-state. Resource limits prevent a runaway build from starving the cluster. `agent any` makes the build non-deterministic and leaks state across builds.
